description: Uses cmd.exe FOR /F loops to extract specific characters from command output or strings by token position and then concatenates them to reconstruct the original command.  The FOR /F loop splits output into tokens and assigns them to loop variables.  Attackers abuse the 'tokens' and 'delims' options to extract individual characters from known strings (like environment variables or command output), effectively building commands character by character from legitimate system data.
native_purpose: cmd.exe FOR /F loop for parsing command output into tokens -- abused to extract individual characters and reconstruct hidden commands
known_behaviors: FOR /F loops with 'tokens=' option extracting specific positions
Multiple nested FOR loops building a command one character at a time
Characters extracted from known strings like %PATH% or 'cmd /c set'
Assembled characters stored in environment variables then executed via call
Output of legitimate commands used as character source material
command_examples: for /f "tokens=3 delims= " %a in ('echo A B whoami') do @%a
for /f "tokens=1-26" %a in ('cmd /c echo w h o a m i') do @set x=%a%b%c%d%e%f&call %x%
cmd /V /C "for /f "tokens=2 delims==" %a in ('set comspec') do @for /f "tokens=1 delims=." %b in ("%a") do @%b"
indicators: FOR /F with 'tokens=' option extracting character positions
Multiple nested FOR /F loops with single-character extraction
Regex: for\s+/[fF]\s+["']tokens=
Characters extracted from environment variable values or command output
SET commands accumulating single characters into a command string

description: Extracts characters from existing environment variables using FOR loops combined with variable substring syntax (%VAR:~offset,len%).  The attacker identifies which characters appear at known positions in standard environment variables (like COMSPEC, PATH, PATHEXT) and uses FOR loops to iterate through these, extracting one character at a time to assemble arbitrary commands.  This is powerful because it uses only built-in environment data -- no suspicious strings appear in the command.
native_purpose: cmd.exe FOR loop combined with environment variable substring extraction -- abused to pull characters from system variables to build hidden commands
known_behaviors: FOR loops iterating over environment variable character positions
%COMSPEC:~N,1% or %PATH:~N,1% extracting single characters
Characters from multiple different environment variables combined
No recognizable command text visible in the obfuscated form
Assembled string executed via CALL or direct expansion
command_examples: set a=%comspec:~14,1%&set b=%comspec:~4,1%&set c=%comspec:~25,1%&set d=%comspec:~0,1%&set e=%comspec:~23,1%&set f=%comspec:~7,1%&call %a%%b%%c%%d%%e%%f%
indicators: Multiple %VAR:~N,1% substring extractions assembling a command
Environment variables like COMSPEC, PATH, PATHEXT used as character sources
Regex: %\w+:~\d+,1% repeated multiple times in a command
No recognizable command keywords in the visible command text
CALL statement executing a variable built from substring extractions

description: Uses the ASSOC or FTYPE commands to enumerate file type associations, then extracts specific characters from their output using FOR /F token parsing.  The ASSOC command lists file extension mappings (e.g., .exe=exefile) and FTYPE lists the command lines for each type.  These outputs contain a rich alphabet of characters that can be mined to reconstruct arbitrary commands.
native_purpose: cmd.exe ASSOC/FTYPE commands listing file associations -- abused as a character source for FOR /F extraction to build hidden commands
known_behaviors: ASSOC or FTYPE output piped into FOR /F for character extraction
Specific token positions selected from association strings
Multiple FOR /F iterations extracting different characters
Assembled command contains no directly readable text
Uses legitimate system configuration data as obfuscation material
command_examples: cmd /V /C "for /f "tokens=2 delims==" %a in ('ftype cmdfile') do @set x=!%a:~0,1!!%a:~3,1!&call !x!"
for /f "tokens=1 delims=." %a in ('assoc .exe') do @for /f "tokens=2 delims==" %b in ('ftype %a') do @echo %b
indicators: ASSOC or FTYPE command output used in FOR /F extraction
Regex: for\s+/[fF].*assoc|for\s+/[fF].*ftype
Token extraction from file type association strings
Characters mined from system configuration output
Combination of ASSOC/FTYPE with substring or token operations

description: Stores the command as a reversed string, then uses a FOR /L loop to iterate through the characters in reverse order, reconstructing the original command.  The reversed string is completely unreadable without reversing it.  The FOR /L loop counts down from the string length to 0, extracting each character using %VAR:~N,1% substring syntax and concatenating in correct order.
native_purpose: cmd.exe FOR /L loop with variable substring extraction -- abused to reverse a backwards-stored command string at runtime
known_behaviors: Command string stored in reverse order in a variable
FOR /L loop counting backwards through the string
%VAR:~N,1% substring extraction at each position
Characters concatenated in reverse order to rebuild the original command
Resulting command executed via CALL or direct expansion with /V:ON
command_examples: cmd /V /C "set r=imaohw&set o=&for /L %i in (5,-1,0) do @set o=!o!!r:~%i,1!&if %i==0 call !o!"
cmd /V /C "set q=ssecorp-teg&set f=&for /L %a in (10,-1,0) do @set f=!f!!q:~%a,1!&if %a==0 call !f!"
set reversed=rid/imaohw&cmd /V /C "set b=&for /L %i in (9,-1,0) do @set b=!b!!reversed:~%i,1!&if %i==0 call !b!"
cmd /V /C "set z=lave/ dmc&set x=&for /L %j in (7,-1,0) do @set x=!x!!z:~%j,1!&if %j==0 !x! echo reversed"
cmd /V /C "set s=resu/dda ten&set t=&for /L %n in (11,-1,0) do @set t=!t!!s:~%n,1!&if %n==0 call !t! testuser password123"
indicators: String variable containing reversed/backwards text
FOR /L loop counting downward (start,-1,0 pattern)
%VAR:~%iterator,1% substring extraction inside the loop
Delayed expansion (!VAR!) used with /V:ON flag
Reversed strings of known commands (imaohw = whoami reversed)

description: Combines command reversal with environment variable extraction to create multi-layer obfuscation.  The reversed command is first constructed from environment variable substrings, then reversed using FOR /L loops.  This means neither the environment variable extraction nor the reversed string individually reveals the command.
native_purpose: cmd.exe multi-layer obfuscation combining environment variable substring extraction with string reversal via FOR /L loops
known_behaviors: Environment variable substrings assembled into a reversed string
FOR /L loop then reverses the assembled string
Two layers of obfuscation: variable extraction + reversal
Final command only visible after both layers are resolved
Heavy use of delayed expansion (!VAR!) and /V:ON flag
command_examples: cmd /V /C "set r=!comspec:~7,1!!comspec:~0,1!!comspec:~23,1!!comspec:~25,1!!comspec:~4,1!!comspec:~14,1!&set o=&for /L %i in (5,-1,0) do @set o=!o!!r:~%i,1!&if %i==0 call !o!"
cmd /V /C "set a=!path:~0,1!!path:~5,1!!comspec:~14,1!&set b=&for /L %z in (2,-1,0) do @set b=!b!!a:~%z,1!&if %z==0 call !b!"
cmd /V /C "set q=!pathext:~0,4!&set w=&for /L %j in (3,-1,0) do @set w=!w!!q:~%j,1!&if %j==0 echo !w!"
indicators: Environment variable substrings assembled into a reversed form
FOR /L descending loop applied to variable-extracted string
Combination of %VAR:~N,1% extraction and reverse-iteration
Regex: set\s+\w+=.*:~\d+,\d+.*for\s+/[lL].*-1
Two-stage obfuscation: extraction then reversal

description: Uses the FINDSTR command to search for specific character patterns in strings, files, or command output, extracting matching characters to assemble a command.  FINDSTR supports regular expressions and literal string matching.  Attackers pipe known content through FINDSTR to extract specific characters at predictable positions, using the filtered output to build command strings.
native_purpose: cmd.exe FINDSTR command for text searching and filtering -- abused to extract specific characters from content for command construction
known_behaviors: FINDSTR used to search/filter strings for specific characters
Output of FINDSTR processed by FOR /F to extract tokens
Regular expression patterns in FINDSTR targeting specific chars
Characters extracted from files, environment variables, or command output
Assembled string executed via CALL or SET+CALL pattern
command_examples: cmd /V /C "set s=abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz&for /f "delims=" %a in ('cmd /c echo !s!^|findstr /r "w"') do @echo found"
indicators: FINDSTR command used to extract or filter characters
FOR /F parsing FINDSTR output for character extraction
Regex: findstr\s+/[rRiI].*in command pipeline
FINDSTR with regex patterns targeting specific character positions
Pipe (|) or caret-escaped pipe (^|) feeding content to FINDSTR

description: Uses FINDSTR to search through files on the system (such as system DLLs, text files in Windows directories, or even the cmd.exe binary itself) to find lines containing specific characters.  The matching lines are then parsed with FOR /F to extract the needed characters.  This technique is especially stealthy because it mines characters from pre-existing system files without writing any data.
native_purpose: cmd.exe FINDSTR file search capability -- abused to mine characters from existing system files for command reconstruction
known_behaviors: FINDSTR searching system files for lines containing target characters
FOR /F extracting specific character positions from matched lines
System files like hosts, protocol, services used as character sources
Binary files searched with /P flag to filter printable characters
No files written -- all characters mined from existing content
indicators: FINDSTR searching system files for character extraction
File paths to Windows system files in FINDSTR commands
Regex: findstr.*C:\\Windows\\|findstr.*/etc/
FOR /F extraction from FINDSTR file search results
System files used as character source: hosts, services, protocol, system.ini

description: Breaks a command into multiple fragments stored in separate environment variables using SET, then concatenates the variables to reconstruct and execute the full command.  Each SET statement is individually harmless, and only the final concatenation reveals the actual command.  This is one of the simplest DOSfuscation techniques and is frequently combined with others.
native_purpose: cmd.exe SET command for environment variable assignment -- abused to split commands into fragments and reassemble via variable expansion
known_behaviors: Multiple SET statements assigning command fragments to variables
Variable references (%a%%b%%c%) concatenating fragments into a command
& operator chaining SET statements together on one line
CALL or direct expansion used to execute the concatenated result
Individual SET values appear harmless in isolation
command_examples: set k=net u&set l=ser /&set m=add test&call %k%%l%%m% P@ss123
indicators: Multiple SET statements followed by concatenated %VAR% expansion
& operator chaining multiple SET assignments
CALL statement executing concatenated environment variables
Short, seemingly meaningless variable values that form a command together

description: Uses cmd.exe delayed expansion (enabled with /V:ON or /V flag) to concatenate variable fragments using !VAR! syntax instead of %VAR%.  Delayed expansion evaluates variables at execution time rather than parse time, allowing dynamic construction of commands within loops and conditional blocks.  This is critical for DOSfuscation because it enables variable values set within a command block to be immediately used in the same block.
native_purpose: cmd.exe delayed expansion (/V:ON) for runtime variable evaluation -- abused to dynamically construct and execute commands within blocks
known_behaviors: cmd /V:ON or cmd /V flag enabling delayed expansion
!VAR! syntax for delayed variable expansion
Variables set and used within the same parenthesized block
Dynamic command construction within FOR loops using !VAR!
Mix of %VAR% (parse-time) and !VAR! (run-time) expansion
indicators: cmd /V:ON or cmd /V flag enabling delayed expansion
!VAR! syntax (exclamation-mark delimited variables)
Regex: cmd\s+/[vV](:ON)?\s+/[cC]
SET followed by !variable! usage in same command chain
Dynamic variable construction with delayed expansion in loops

description: Assigns command fragments to variables in a deliberately jumbled order, then concatenates them in the correct sequence to produce the command.  The SET statements appear in a random order making it harder to visually reconstruct the command.  Variable names may also be randomized or use misleading names to further obscure intent.
native_purpose: cmd.exe SET with out-of-order variable assignment -- abused to fragment and reorder command parts for obfuscation
known_behaviors: SET assignments in scrambled order (not matching command sequence)
Variable names chosen to mislead (randomized or counterintuitive)
Final concatenation reorders fragments into correct command sequence
May use numeric or alphabetic variable names in non-sequential order
Execution via CALL or direct %var% expansion in correct order
indicators: SET assignments in non-sequential order that reassemble to a command
Variable names that don't match their content semantically
Multiple short SET values that individually appear meaningless
CALL with concatenated variables in different order than SET statements

description: Inserts caret (^) characters throughout command tokens.  In cmd.exe, the caret is the escape character and is stripped during command parsing (except before special characters).  This means w^h^o^a^m^i executes identically to whoami.  Attackers insert carets to break up recognizable command strings, defeating signature-based detection that relies on exact string matching.
native_purpose: cmd.exe caret (^) escape character for special character escaping -- abused by inserting between normal characters to break string signatures
known_behaviors: Carets (^) inserted between normal alphabetic characters
cmd.exe strips carets during parsing, executing the original command
Every other character may be a caret in heavy obfuscation
Carets break up keywords that detection rules look for
Does not affect command execution -- purely cosmetic obfuscation
indicators: Excessive caret (^) characters between normal alphabetic characters
Regex: \w\^\w\^\w (letter-caret-letter pattern repeated)
Known commands with carets inserted: w^h^o^a^m^i, n^e^t, p^o^w^e^r
Caret density significantly higher than normal command text
Command still executes normally after removing all carets

description: Maximally inserts carets by placing one between every single character in the command.  This creates an extreme visual obfuscation where the original command is nearly unreadable.  Combined with other techniques (like concatenation or variable expansion), heavy caret insertion creates deeply layered obfuscation that is difficult to analyze both visually and programmatically.
native_purpose: cmd.exe caret (^) character applied maximally -- every inter-character position gets a caret for maximum visual obfuscation
known_behaviors: Every character separated by a caret in the command string
Command length roughly doubles due to inserted carets
Combined with other obfuscation layers for compound obfuscation
Creates very long command lines that are visually impenetrable
Still executes identically to the un-careted original
indicators: Nearly every character separated by ^ (caret-to-character ratio > 0.4)
Regex: (\w\^){4,} (4+ consecutive letter-caret sequences)
Command string length is roughly double expected length
Removing all ^ characters yields a recognizable command
Caret appears even between spaces and special characters

description: Uses the %VAR:~start,length% substring syntax to extract characters from environment variables.  Every environment variable on a Windows system contains characters that can be extracted by position.  Common sources include COMSPEC (C:\Windows\system32\cmd.exe), PATH, PATHEXT, and others.  By selecting the right offsets, any ASCII character present in environment variables can be extracted to build commands.
native_purpose: cmd.exe %VAR:~start,length% substring syntax for extracting parts of environment variables -- abused to mine individual characters for command construction
known_behaviors: Multiple %VAR:~N,1% extractions pulling single characters
Characters from COMSPEC, PATH, PATHEXT, WINDIR, and other env vars
Extracted characters concatenated to form command strings
No command keywords visible in the obfuscated text
Negative offsets (%VAR:~-1%) used to count from end of variable
indicators: Multiple %VAR:~N,1% or !VAR:~N,1! substrings extracting single characters
Environment variable names like COMSPEC, PATH, PATHEXT in substring syntax
No readable command keywords -- all characters from variable offsets
Negative offsets in substring syntax (%VAR:~-N,1%)

description: Uses the %VAR:old=new% substitution syntax to transform environment variable content.  This syntax replaces all occurrences of 'old' with 'new' in the variable value.  Attackers set a variable to a value containing placeholder characters, then use substitution to transform it into the intended command.  Multiple substitutions can be chained to progressively transform a string.
native_purpose: cmd.exe %VAR:old=new% string substitution in environment variables -- abused to transform benign-looking strings into commands
known_behaviors: %VAR:old=new% syntax replacing characters in variable values
Multiple chained substitutions transforming content step by step
Initial variable value appears benign but transforms to a command
Substitution can remove, add, or change characters
Combined with SET to create the initial value before substitution
indicators: %VAR:pattern=% or %VAR:pattern=replacement% substitution syntax
SET creating a variable with repeating garbage characters
Substitution removing placeholder characters to reveal a command
Multiple %VAR:X=Y% substitutions in a single command line

description: Replaces spaces between command arguments with commas (,) or semicolons (;).  In cmd.exe, commas and semicolons are treated as valid argument separators equivalent to spaces in many contexts.  This means 'dir,C:\' and 'dir;C:\' are equivalent to 'dir C:\'.  This simple substitution can defeat detection rules that expect space-separated arguments.
native_purpose: cmd.exe comma and semicolon as alternate argument delimiters -- abused to replace spaces and break expected command argument patterns
known_behaviors: Commas or semicolons used instead of spaces between arguments
cmd.exe treats , and ; as argument separators like spaces
Command structure appears unusual but executes normally
Can be combined with other obfuscation for layered effect
Particularly effective in breaking regex-based detection rules
indicators: Commas or semicolons used as argument separators instead of spaces
Regex: \w+[,;]+/[cCkK][,;]+\w+ (cmd,/c,command pattern)
Multiple consecutive commas or semicolons between arguments
Known commands with , or ; where spaces are expected
Unusual comma/semicolon density in command-line arguments

description: Wraps commands or command components in unnecessary parentheses () to alter the visual structure and break pattern-matching detection.  In cmd.exe, parentheses create command groups but do not change execution semantics for single commands.  Deeply nested or excessive parentheses create visual noise that obscures the actual command.  This is frequently combined with other obfuscation techniques.
native_purpose: cmd.exe parentheses () for command grouping and multi-line blocks -- abused to add unnecessary nesting that obscures command structure
known_behaviors: Excessive or unnecessary parentheses wrapping commands
Deeply nested parentheses creating visual obfuscation
Parentheses combined with & or | operators for compound grouping
Single commands wrapped in multiple layers of parentheses
No change in execution behavior -- purely visual obfuscation
indicators: Excessive parentheses wrapping commands (3+ nested levels)
Regex: \({2,}\w+\){2,} (multiple nested parens around a word)
Parentheses around single commands that don't need grouping
Parentheses combined with comma/semicolon substitution
Unusual parenthesis density in command line

description: Uses %% (double percent) for variable references in batch file (.bat/.cmd) context instead of single % used in interactive cmd.exe.  In batch files, FOR loop variables use %%a instead of %a, and environment variables can be referenced with extra percent signs.  Attackers exploit the difference between interactive and batch contexts to create commands that only work in one context, or to add extra percent signs that create confusion in analysis tools.
native_purpose: cmd.exe batch file double-percent syntax for FOR variables and variable escaping -- abused to add confusion between interactive and batch file contexts
known_behaviors: %%VAR%% syntax in batch files instead of %VAR%
FOR loop variables use %%a in batch, %a in interactive
Extra % characters that get stripped during batch processing
Context-dependent behavior confuses analysis tools
Mixing %% and % for deliberate confusion
indicators: Double percent (%%) in variable references outside batch context
FOR loop variables with %% in command-line (batch syntax)
Regex: %%\w+%% or for\s+/[fFlL]\s+%%\w+
Mixing of % and %% variable references in same command
Extra percent signs that appear unnecessary for the context

description: Combines caret insertion with environment variable concatenation for multi-layer obfuscation.  Each SET command has carets inserted in both the variable names and values, and the final CALL also contains carets.  This requires a defender to strip carets AND resolve variable concatenation to recover the original command.
native_purpose: cmd.exe compound obfuscation combining caret escape insertion with SET-based variable concatenation for multi-layer defense evasion
known_behaviors: SET commands with carets in variable names and/or values
CALL statements with carets in variable references
Two obfuscation layers must be resolved to see original command
Carets in SET values do not affect the stored value
Combined visual and structural obfuscation
indicators: SET or CALL commands with carets (^) inserted between characters
Both caret insertion and variable concatenation in same command
Regex: s\^e\^t|c\^a\^l\^l (careted SET or CALL keywords)
Carets in variable values that get stripped by cmd.exe
Multiple obfuscation layers requiring sequential de-obfuscation

description: Combines SET-based concatenation with %VAR:~N,L% substring extraction for multi-layer obfuscation.  First, environment variable substrings are extracted and stored in intermediate variables.  Then those intermediate variables are concatenated to form the final command.  Neither the substring extractions nor the concatenation individually reveals the command.
native_purpose: cmd.exe compound obfuscation combining variable concatenation with substring extraction for double-layer command hiding
known_behaviors: Environment variable substrings stored in intermediate SET variables
Intermediate variables concatenated to form the command
Two resolution steps needed: substring extraction then concatenation
Original command completely hidden from static analysis
Uses both %VAR:~N,1% syntax and %VAR% concatenation
command_examples: set a=%comspec:~14,1%&set b=%comspec:~4,1%&set c=%comspec:~25,1%&set d=%comspec:~0,1%&set e=%comspec:~23,1%&set f=%comspec:~7,1%&call %a%%b%%c%%d%%e%%f%
indicators: SET assignments using %VAR:~N,L% followed by CALL with variable concat
Both substring extraction and concatenation in same command chain
Multiple SET commands extracting single characters then combining
Regex: set\s+\w+=%\w+:~\d+,\d+%.*call\s+%\w+%%\w+%
No readable command text in any individual SET or extraction

description: Applies multiple DOSfuscation techniques simultaneously: caret insertion, variable concatenation, substring extraction, comma/semicolon substitution, and parentheses grouping all in a single command.  This represents the maximum obfuscation achievable with DOSfuscation and requires resolving all layers to recover the original command.  Real-world attackers often combine 2-3 techniques; combining all techniques is rare but possible.
native_purpose: cmd.exe maximum compound obfuscation stacking all available DOSfuscation techniques for deep defense evasion
known_behaviors: Multiple DOSfuscation techniques applied simultaneously
Carets, variable concat, substring extraction, delimiters all present
Extremely long and visually impenetrable command lines
Requires sequential de-obfuscation of each layer
Highest evasion rate against signature-based detection
indicators: Three or more DOSfuscation techniques combined in a single command
Presence of carets AND variable expansion AND delimiter substitution
Regex: \^.*%\w+:~\d+.*[,;].*call (caret + expansion + delimiter)
Extremely long cmd.exe command lines (500+ characters)
Command requires multi-step de-obfuscation pipeline to analyze
Nested parentheses combined with carets and variable references
